verdicts.reasoning:
Both answers are strong, well-structured, and hit all six criteria: they characterize Chinchilla/compute-optimal scaling, address the data wall (~300T tokens, Epoch), treat inference-time compute as a distinct axis, cite specific evidence, take a clear position, and steelman a counterargument. The decision rests on correctness of the specific claims, not length.

Criterion 4 (accurate evidence without fabrication) separates them. Answer A explicitly diagnoses and corrects a real conflation: the "10x" figure is a pretraining-substitution factor, NOT a 10x cut in GPT-5's total budget, and it gives Epoch's actual estimates (GPT-5 ~5e25, GPT-4.5 >1e26, GPT-4 ~2e25 FLOP) so GPT-5 used ~half of GPT-4.5 but still more than GPT-4. Answer B leans on Karpathy's "10x compute per 0.5 version" heuristic as its central pretraining datapoint and, while it commendably flags it as directional in a caveat, still uses it as the load-bearing "cleanest natural experiment." A's handling of this specific compute relationship is more precise and less prone to the exact misreading A warns against.

A also adds a genuinely important and accurate nuance B lacks: the Epoch April-2024 capability breakpoint PRECEDES o1's release, so it reflects broad RL/post-training investment rather than reasoning-model releases alone — this materially sharpens the causal story and is correct. A's counterargument (b) — that the acceleration may be a one-time harvest of latent pretraining slack concentrated in verifiable domains, restating Sutskever's "generalization is inadequate" — is the stronger, more threatening steelman and is internally consistent with the evidence presented; it also specifies a decisive near-term test (GPT-6-class runs bounded by the same token stock). B's counterargument is solid (benchmark saturation vs capability, complements not substitutes, "we've been here before") but somewhat less incisive.

On criterion 6, both acknowledge uncertainty; B is admirably explicit with its caveats block distinguishing heuristics from measured constants (a real strength), but A also names the specific empirical test that would flip its verdict.

B has some looser claims presented more confidently than A (e.g., ARC-AGI-1 87.5% and FrontierMath figures via aggregator pages, inverse-scaling thresholds), which B partly hedges. Neither contains an outright fabrication I can identify. Net: A is marginally more correct on the most load-bearing factual point (the GPT-5/GPT-4.5 compute relationship and the "10x" interpretation) and adds a correct, non-trivial breakpoint nuance, giving it the edge on genuine correctness rather than on length — indeed A is longer but that is not the basis for the verdict.

Both answers hit all six criteria: they accurately characterize Chinchilla/compute-optimal pretraining, address the ~300T-token data wall (~2028), treat inference/test-time scaling as a distinct axis, take a clear "axis-rotation, not plateau" position, steelman a counterargument, and note uncertainty. The verdict turns on factual precision and fabrication risk.

Criterion 4 (cites evidence accurately without fabrication) is where they separate. Answer A makes a materially misleading claim: it repeatedly implies GPT-4.5 was ~10x pretraining compute over the prior version and frames GPT-5<GPT-4.5 as a clean "retreat," anchored on Karpathy's "10x per 0.5 version" heuristic. Answer B correctly diagnoses and corrects the exact confusion A embodies: the "10x" is a post-training substitution factor (post-training can substitute for ~10x pretraining compute at equal performance), NOT that GPT-5's total was 10x smaller — GPT-5 (~5e25) was roughly half of GPT-4.5 (>1e26) and still ~2x GPT-4 (~2e25). This is a real, load-bearing factual distinction that A gets wrong (or at least conflates) and B gets right and explicitly flags. B also handles the Epoch April-2024 breakpoint with the correct and non-obvious nuance that it PRECEDES o1, so it reflects broad RL/post-training investment rather than reasoning-model releases alone — a subtle point A misses.

On criterion 5/6, B's counterargument is stronger and more decisive: it distinguishes (a) "the distinction is analytically meaningless / total system compute is one power law" from (b) the sharper "acceleration is a one-time harvest of latent pretraining slack concentrated in verifiable domains; general capability actually plateaued," with a concrete falsification test (GPT-6-class runs bounded by the same data stock failing to lift the base ceiling). A's counterargument is good but its "we've been here before / benchmark saturation" framing partly re-uses points already in its main body and rests on the same 10x-misframed GPT-5 datapoint.

A has one honesty advantage: it explicitly caveats that "accuracy collapse at 2-3x" and "10x per 0.5 version" are directional heuristics, not canonical constants — good epistemic hygiene. But A still deploys those figures as load-bearing in its argument despite the caveat, whereas B avoids the error entirely by stating the compute numbers precisely.

Both risk citing some 2026-dated/forward sources (Lilian Weng June 2026 post, aggregator benchmark pages in A; capex/ARC-AGI-3 figures in B) that may be soft, so neither is clean on that dimension. On balance, B is more correct on the single most concrete, checkable technical claim in the task (the GPT-5/GPT-4.5/GPT-4 compute relationship and the meaning of "10x"), which A gets misleadingly wrong. This is a correctness edge, not a length edge — B is longer but the decisive factor is accuracy of a specific claim, so lengthDriven is false.

Both answers cover all six criteria competently and land in the same defensible position ("scaling didn't plateau, the axis rotated"), with strong steelmanned counterarguments and explicit uncertainty conditions. The decisive question is genuine correctness of the load-bearing claims.

Criterion 1 (Chinchilla/compute-optimal): Both accurate. A gives the L(C) power-law form and the ~20 tokens/param ratio, correctly notes Chinchilla corrected Kaplan's over-scaling of params. B also cites ~20:1 and correctly describes the Kaplan-to-Chinchilla correction. Tie.

Criterion 2 (data wall): Both accurate on Villalobos/Epoch. A: high-quality text ~2026, public stock median ~2028, very likely by 2032. B: ~300T tokens, exhaust ~2028 (80% CI 2026-2032). Both correct; B adds the ~300T figure and CI. Both correctly cite Muennighoff (~4 epochs ~free, decaying by ~16). Tie.

Criterion 3 (test-time compute): Both treat it as a distinct axis with log-linear/power-law gains, and both correctly flag it as contested/showing ceilings (inverse scaling, verifiability limits, recurring per-query cost). Tie on substance.

Criterion 4 (evidence without fabrication) is where the discriminator lies. A stays at the level of well-established results (Hoffmann, Villalobos, Muennighoff, o1/o3/R1) and hedges the contested test-time-law point — low fabrication risk. B is far more specific and thus carries more fabrication exposure: it cites arXiv IDs (2509.23963 Chinchilla robustness, 2507.07931 "Meek Models"), a June-2026 "Lilian Weng: Scaling Laws, Carefully" post, specific benchmark numbers (o3 87.5% ARC-AGI-1, FrontierMath 2%->25%, 57M-token/14-min answer), Karpathy's "10x per 0.5 version," and Epoch's "GPT-5 used less compute than GPT-4.5." Several of these are plausibly real (the ARC-AGI o3 result, Epoch's 4-5x/yr, Villalobos), but several are the kind of precise, recent, attributable claims that are high-risk to assert as fact (the specific arXiv numbers, a dated Weng post, exact token/latency figures). Critically, B itself flags in its caveats that the "accuracy-collapse at 2-3x" and "10x per 0.5 version" are directional heuristics from secondary/individual sources, and that several 2026-dated figures come from aggregators and are only "indicative." That transparency is good scientific practice and mitigates the fabrication penalty — but it does not fully neutralize it, because the honest labeling is applied to some claims while other equally-specific attributions (arXiv IDs, the Weng post, exact benchmark deltas) are presented as settled without independent verification available to me.

Weighing it: A is safer and cleaner on Criterion 4 (fewer unverifiable specifics, appropriately hedged), while B is richer and, IF its citations are accurate, more evidentially grounded — but B assumes correctness risk that A avoids, only partially offset by its caveats. On the other criteria they are genuinely even. A's extra precision on the counterargument (transfer-to-non-verifiable as the single load-bearing bet) and B's extra precision on revealed-preference evidence (GPT-5<GPT-4.5 compute) roughly offset. Neither has a clear, verifiable correctness edge: A trades evidential specificity for lower error risk; B trades higher error risk for more concrete grounding, with self-aware hedging. This nets to a tie on genuine correctness. The verdict is not length-driven — B is longer, but I am not crediting it for length; if anything its extra specifics are a wash between added evidence and added fabrication exposure.

Both answers are strong and structurally near-identical: same three-axis decomposition, same core thesis ("scaling migrated/rotated, didn't plateau"), same steelmanned counterargument (relabeling hides deceleration; test-time gains confined to verifiable domains), and both hold their position while conceding the transfer question.

Criterion-by-criterion:

C1 (pretraining/compute-optimal): Both accurate. A cites a Chinchilla robustness re-analysis (arXiv 2509.23963) and 20:1 ratio. B gives the loss power-law form L(C)≈L∞+a·C^(−α), notes Chinchilla corrected Kaplan's over-scaling of params vs data, and flags the ~20 t/param with a caveat that later work suggests ~10-12. Both correct on the "loss law intact, exponent small/economic returns bend" framing. B's L∞ + a·C^(−α) is a defensible standard form. Tie.

C2 (data wall): Both accurate. A: ~300T tokens, exhaustion ~2028, Muennighoff ~4 epochs, synthetic-in-verifiable-domains, model-collapse risk. B: high-quality text ~2026, public stock median ~2028 (likely by 2032), Muennighoff ~4 epochs negligible / ~16 epochs to zero, synthetic + collapse risk. Both cite Villalobos/Epoch correctly. B's ~16-epoch detail is a genuine, accurate additional specific. Tie / very slight edge B on data precision.

C3 (inference-time): Both treat it as a distinct axis with real gains and built-in ceilings (verifiability, per-query recurring cost, inverse scaling). A adds concrete numbers: o3 87.5% ARC-AGI-1, FrontierMath ~2%→~25%. B adds the nuance that test-time scaling laws are contested/piecewise. Both good.

C4 (evidence without fabrication): This is where risk lives. A packs many specific figures (GPT-5 < GPT-4.5 compute, 4-5x/yr, 3x/yr efficiency, o3 ARC-AGI numbers) and provides a source list — but several sources are 2026-dated and unverifiable (Lilian Weng "Scaling Laws, Carefully" June 2026, a "meek models" arXiv, an inverse-scaling blog), and some figures (Karpathy 10x/0.5-version, "accuracy collapse at 2-3x") are heuristics. Critically, A EXPLICITLY flags these as directional/illustrative/non-canonical in both a preamble and a closing caveat — good epistemic hygiene that reduces the fabrication penalty. B uses fewer hard numbers, attaches them to canonical citable sources (Kaplan 2020, Hoffmann 2022, Villalobos, Muennighoff), and its riskier specifics ("~16x samples for a fixed accuracy step") are hedged. B's lower specificity means lower fabrication surface; A's higher specificity is partly de-risked by explicit caveats. Roughly a wash — A gives more verifiable-if-true evidence but carries more unverifiable citations; B is safer but thinner on quantified results.

C5 (clear position + steelman): Both nail it. Both take "not plateaued / axis rotated" and both write a genuinely strong, non-strawman counterargument, then explain why they still hold. Tie.

C6 (uncertainty / what would change the answer): B is slightly stronger here — it explicitly names the single load-bearing bet ("whether reasoning-RL/test-time generalizes beyond verifiable domains") and states the condition under which the position "ages badly" (transfer fails AND synthetic hits collapse AND no new axis). A also acknowledges uncertainty and labels its counterargument's strongest form a "forward-looking bet," and flags contested claims, but B's conditional falsification is crisper. Slight edge B.

Netting out: A's advantage is more concrete, quantified evidence (with honest caveats); B's advantage is tighter falsification conditions and safer sourcing. These roughly offset. Neither has a disqualifying factual error. The length/completeness difference is modest and not the deciding factor — both are complete on all six criteria. Genuine tie on correctness.

Both answers are technically excellent and hit all six success criteria accurately, with no material correctness errors in either.

Criterion 1 (WebAuthn mitigations): Both correctly state origin/RP-ID binding defeats phishing, no shared secret defeats stuffing/reuse, public-key-only storage defeats DB breach, single-use challenges defeat replay. Both also correctly note the server-side verification obligations (validate origin, rpIdHash, challenge single-use/expiry, UV flag). Accurate in both. A adds the nuance that evilginx-style proxy phishing is specifically broken by origin binding while a bespoke relay/session theft is not — a correct and important distinction. B makes the same point in section A/B. Tie.

Criterion 2 (account recovery as primary residual): Both explicitly name recovery/fallback as the weakest link where attackers pivot. A calls it the "single highest-value place to invest"; B calls it "the weakest link and the primary phishing target." Both correct. Tie.

Criterion 3 (device loss / sync-fabric compromise): Both cover synced passkeys shifting the trust root to the Apple ID/Google account and that compromising that account inherits all passkeys. Both cover caBLE/hybrid QR social-engineering. B adds a genuinely correct and subtle technical point A omits: synced passkeys legitimately report 0/non-incrementing signature counters, so counter-regression clone detection is largely unusable for consumer passkeys (log, don't hard-block). A mentions checking signCount for cloned-authenticator detection without this caveat — not wrong, but B is more precise here. Slight edge B on this sub-point.

Criterion 4 (registration-time and session-after-auth): Both cover registration/enrollment binding (attacker self-enrolls a passkey → durable access) and post-auth session risks (cookie theft, XSS, session fixation/CSRF, malware). Both accurate. A explicitly calls out CSRF and session fixation by name; B is slightly terser but complete. Tie.

Criterion 5 (mitigated vs residual distinction): Both draw the line cleanly and correctly — WebAuthn secures the login ceremony/assertion, not the adjacent surface. Neither overclaims. A is more emphatic that AiTM-as-a-category is not eliminated. Both accurate. Tie.

Criterion 6 (concrete defenses): Both propose concrete, prioritized, correct defenses: harden recovery (cooldown, out-of-band notify, no silent SMS/email downgrade), gate enrollment behind step-up, notify-on-sensitive-event with kill switch, session binding (DPoP/token-binding, short TTL, revoke-everywhere), step-up for high-value actions, server-side ceremony hygiene, downgrade hardening, enumeration resistance, XSS defense, multi-passkey enrollment to reduce recovery reliance. Both name real libraries/mechanisms correctly (B names SimpleWebAuthn/webauthn4j; A cites Trusted-Types-adjacent CSP/frame-ancestors). Tie.

Prosecutor pass: No factual errors found in either. A's claim that platform authenticators "generally don't rate-limit repeated biometric attempts" is a minor overstatement (many do fall back to PIN/lockout), but it's framed as a physical/coercion edge case and doesn't materially mislead.
